classification.title: EDUCATIONAL PROVISIONS OF THE STATE CONSTITUTION OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky State Constitution outlines the framework for common schools, mandating the General Assembly to provide an efficient system. It details the inviolable nature of a bond and stock in the Bank of Kentucky designated for common school funding, with interest and dividends to be appropriated solely for this purpose. It also specifies that no additional taxes for education beyond common schools can be raised without voter approval, with an exception for existing taxes for educational purposes and the Agricultural and Mechanical College.

classification.title: COUNTY SUPERINTENDENT
classification.summary: This statute outlines the responsibilities and powers of county superintendents in Kentucky's common school system. It details election procedures, bond requirements, and the superintendent's authority to lay off, abolish, or consolidate school districts, condemn unfit schoolhouses and furniture, and make official visits. Notably, it includes a provision for counties with a city of the first class maintaining a separate school system, stating that the county superintendent must reside outside such city and be elected by voters residing outside the city. This implies a potential for separate educational systems based on urban/rural distinctions, which could have been used to segregate Black students if such separate systems were racially divided.

This statute's language, while not naming race, creates a framework that could facilitate or reflect such segregation. classification.title: COUNTY BOARD OF EXAMINERS
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties of county boards of examiners in Kentucky regarding teacher certification. It specifies that county certificates shall be granted to persons not under eighteen years of age upon written examinations. Crucially, it mandates separate examination dates for white teachers (third Friday and Saturday of May, June, July, and August) and colored teachers (fourth Friday and Saturday of the same months). The State Board of Examiners is also required to prepare separate series of questions for white and colored teachers, though all questions are to be of the same grade.

classification.title: 36 COUNTY BOARD OF EXAMINERS
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for county boards of examiners in administering teacher examinations. It details the handling of examination questions, the requirements for applicants (including moral character and age), and the conduct of the examination itself to ensure fairness and prevent cheating. The statute specifies that examiners must be satisfied with an applicant's moral character and age, and will not grant certificates to those who indulge in drunkenness, profanity, gambling, or licentiousness, or who have had improper access to examination questions. It also mandates that only applicants be present during the examination and that they be seated at a proper distance from each other.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the administrative procedures for teacher examinations in Kentucky common schools. It focuses on the integrity of the examination process, the qualifications of applicants (moral character, age), and the conduct of the examiners. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The requirements for moral character and age are applied universally to all applicants. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Kentucky Common School Laws - 1896
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky Common School Laws from 1896 outlines regulations for school districts, including size limitations, schoolhouse location, record-keeping by county superintendents, the formation of districts across county lines, and the privileges of adjoining districts regarding student attendance and tuition. It details procedures for reporting and drawing funds for districts that span county boundaries and establishes conditions for student transfers between adjoining districts, requiring written consent from trustees and parents, and ensuring no negative impact on school terms or teacher contracts.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and operational aspects of common school districts in Kentucky in 1896. It covers topics such as district size, location of schoolhouses, record-keeping, and the process for creating districts that span county lines. It also addresses the ability of children in adjoining districts to attend the most convenient schoolhouse and the associated tuition arrangements. §72.[District Trustees - Authority , Number ,
Qualifications , Election - Chairman of Board .]-
Each school districtshall be under the control of three trustees,
who shallbe of good moral character, at leasttwenty -one years of
age, and for white schools, able to read and write, and for colored
schools,able to read and write,ifpracticable,one of whom shallbe
electedeach year,for the term of three years,to fillthe place of the
trusteegoing out of office. No person holding the officeof trustee
of any private school shall be eligibleto hold the officeof trustee
of any common school. The vote in electinga trusteeshallbe taken
viva voce, and the electionshall be held at the schoolhouse, and if
no schoolhouse be in the district, at such convenient place as the
trustees may select, from one o'clocktillsix o'clockin the after-
noon on the firstSaturday of June in each year,noticethereof hav-
ing been posted by the trustees at three of the most public places
in the districtfor ten days immediately preceding the day of
election. At this election the qualified voters of the district
shall be the electors; and any widow having a child between six
and twenty -years of age, and any widow or spinsterhaving a ward
between the ages of six and twenty years may also vote. The
officersof the electionshall be a judge and a clerk, who shall be
residentsof the district,and be chosen by the voters at the opening
of the polls. The judge of electionshall give the casting vote in
case of a tie, provided he has not heretofore voted , and give a cer-
tificateof electionto the person elected, signed by himself, and
report the trusteesthus elected,in writing, to the county superin-
tendent of the county within fivedays afterthe day of election. In
case of a tie, in which the judge has voted, then he shall certify
the fact to the county superintendent, and the county superinten-
dent shall appoint the trustee, and give him a certificate, and the
trustee so selectedshall act until his successor is duly qualified.
The trustee so elected shall hold officefor three years , from the
firstday of July succeeding his election, and untilhis successor is
duly elected or appointed and qualified. Any two of the trustees
may constitutea quorum to transactthe businesspertainingto their
office, at a meeting of which all shall have had reasonable notice.
The trusteehaving the shortesttime to serve shallbe chairman of
the board of trustees, whose duty it shallbe to preside at allits
meetings , and to make the reports, and to perform all other such

classification.title: District Trustees - Authority, Number, Qualifications, Election - Chairman of Board
classification.summary: This statute outlines the qualifications and election process for district trustees in common schools. It specifies that trustees must be of good moral character and at least twenty-one years of age. Notably, it states that for white schools, trustees must be able to read and write, while for colored schools, this ability is required 'if practicable.' The confidence is high because the racial distinction is explicit and directly impacts the qualifications for a position within the school system. §77. [Securing Site, Erecting and Locating
Schoolhouse , etc .]— The trustees, with the consent of the
countysuperintendent,may take land,by purchaseor donation,for
the purpose of erecting thereon a schoolhouse, provide for and
secure the erection of the same , construct such out-buildings and
inclosuresas shall be conducive to the protection of the property
and the comfort and decency of the pupils and teachers; make re-
pairs and provide the necessary furniture and apparatus ; but the
said trusteesshallnot buy for such use property in which they are
either directlyor indirectlyinterested. They shallhave power to
recover for any damages that may be done the property in their
charge, and they may change the locationof the schoolhouse, sell
or dispose of the old site,and use the proceeds thereof towards pro-
curinga new one. If they can not agree with the owner of any
land most suitablefor a schoolhouse site as to the price and terms
of purchase and sale thereof, they may have the same condemned
in the manner provided for condemning lands for the use of rail-
road companies ; and cause a deed to the land by a commissioner
to be made to the trustees and their successors ; which deed shall
be duly acknowledged and recorded in the officeof the clerk of
the county court,and thereupon the fee-simple titleto said lotshall
vest in said trusteesand their successors in office; but they shall
not have the right to condemn any private property which is used
by the owner as a residence, garden , orchard or burying ground .
The quantity of land thus condemned shall in no case exceed one
acre. A majority of the school electors of any district, within
thirtydays afterthe locationor sitehas been selectedby the trus-
tees, may appeal from the decision of the trustees in the location
of the schoolhouse, or sitefor the same ,to the county superinten-
dent of the county, whose decision in the case shall be final. In
such location it shall be made as near as practicable to the center
of the district as will be convenient of access to all the inhabitants
of the district;and the trusteesshallmake provisionsfor such roads
or passways to the schoolhouse as willaccommodate allthe pupils
who may be entitledto attend, and may apply to the county court
having jurisdictionto open the same as other roadways are opened
for public necessityand convenience. But where there is no road

classification.title: GRADED COMMON SCHOOLS
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky Common School Laws from 1896 outlines the conditions for establishing graded common school districts. It specifies that the proposition to establish such a school must be voted on by "legal white voters" and details the types of taxes (ad valorem and poll tax) that can be levied on "said white voters" and "each white male inhabitant over twenty-one (21) years of age residing in said proposed graded common school district." The law also mentions that provisions of the act shall apply to graded free school districts organized by "colored people of this Commonwealth," implying a separate but potentially unequal system.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly restricts voting on the establishment of graded common schools and the levying of taxes to "legal white voters" and "white male inhabitants." This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. While it mentions provisions applying to "colored people," the primary mechanism described for establishing and funding these schools is explicitly for white residents. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with explicit racial indicators in the education category.

classification.title: GRADED COMMON SCHOOLS
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky Common School Laws from 1896 outlines provisions for graded common schools. It details the management of sinking funds for bond payments, the governance of assessment and collection by Section 79, and vests title and power over common school and county seminary property in the board of trustees. It also specifies conditions for the use of county seminary property, requiring equitable reduced tuition for all pupils of the county, and allows for the conveyance of existing school property to the graded school trustees. Finally, it describes the process for appointing election officers for future elections after the initial organization of the graded common school.

classification.title: Colored Graded Free Schools
classification.summary: This statute explicitly mandates the segregation of public schools based on race. It states that provisions for graded common schools apply to districts organized by colored people, with the word 'colored' replacing 'white' where applicable. Crucially, it prohibits white individuals from voting in elections for colored schools, prevents the taxation of white property for colored schools, and vice versa. classification.title: TEACHERS' INSTITUTE.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for county superintendents to organize and hold annual teachers' institutes. It specifies the duration (5-10 days), the timeframe for holding them (July 1 to November 1), the development of a program and syllabus by the State Superintendent and State Board of Examiners, and penalties for non-compliance. It also details the duties of superintendents in employing conductors, managing attendance, and reporting on the institute. The statute mandates that all subjects related to the teacher's work must be exhibited at each session.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: TEACHERS' INSTITUTE
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements and procedures for teachers' institutes in Kentucky common schools. It details the employment of institute conductors, the convention of institute conductors for program development, and the mandatory attendance of teachers at these institutes. Penalties for non-attendance are also specified, with exceptions for sickness or disability. The statute also states that after an institute is held, no certificate to teach can be granted unless the applicant has attended the full session of an institute.

classification.title: COUNTY AND DISTRICT LIBRARY
classification.summary: This section establishes a county teachers' library in each county, to be kept in the office of the county superintendent. The library is for the exclusive use and benefit of the teachers of the county. Funds for the library are to be collected at annual institutes and from donations. A county library committee, consisting of the superintendent and two elected persons, will manage the library and report to the Superintendent of Public Instruction. The library is to be organized in connection with a district school, with trustees accountable for its care and management. The library is free to all pupils of suitable age belonging to the district, with conditions for membership for other residents.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the establishment and management of a county teachers' library. It outlines the funding sources, the composition of the library committee, and the accessibility of the library to students and residents. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines provisions for colored schools in Kentucky, including the management of donations for their aid, the age range for colored pupils, and the separate designation of colored school districts. It mandates that census data for colored children be taken and reported similarly to white children and that colored school districts be identified alphabetically.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly refers to 'COLORED SCHOOLS' and 'colored children' multiple times. Section 153 states that the census of colored children shall be taken and reported 'at the same time and in the same manner as required by law for taking the census of white children.' Furthermore, it mandates that 'All colored districts shall be designated by letters in alphabetical order for each county, as District A, District B, and so on.' This clearly indicates a system of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it appears to be part of a compilation of laws, likely a revised code or similar compilation, given the numbering and structure (e.g., § 152, § 153).

classification.summary: This statute mandates the election of colored school trustees in the same manner as white trustees. It explicitly prohibits the taxation of one race for the benefit of the other's schools and forbids individuals of one race from voting for trustees of the other race's schools. classification.title: AN ACT concerning the Education of Children.
classification.summary: This act mandates that parents or guardians send children between the ages of 7 and 14 to school for at least eight consecutive weeks annually. Exemptions are provided for home-schooled children, those with physical or mental conditions, poverty preventing proper clothing, those who have already acquired the required branches of study, or if there is no school of the child's race within two miles by the nearest traveled road. Penalties are imposed for non-compliance.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white school, in the case of white children, or colored school, in the case of colored children' as a condition for exemption from compulsory attendance. This clearly indicates a racially segregated educational system, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The law mandates attendance but also provides an exemption if a school of the child's race is not available within a certain distance, thereby reinforcing segregation. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law in the education category.

classification.title: ENFORCED ATTENDANCE
classification.summary: This statute outlines the enforcement of compulsory school attendance laws. It details penalties for parents or guardians who fail to ensure their children attend school, including fines and legal action. Crucially, Section 164 explicitly states that the conditions and provisions of the act apply to parents, guardians, or persons having control of colored children in the same manner as white children. However, it also includes a segregation clause: 'but no white child shall be permitted to attend or become a pupil in any school for colored children, and no colored child shall be'. This section directly mandates racial segregation in schools.
classification.reasoning: The statute is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow because Section 164 explicitly mandates racial segregation in schools by stating that white children cannot attend colored schools and colored children cannot attend white schools. ive districts; and whereas , the present rate of taxation authorized
by law is wholly insufficientto pay offand discharge such indebt-

Be itenactedby the GeneralAssembly of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky: § 1. That the trusteesof allcommon school districtsin this
State,in which said trusteeshave heretoforeborrowed or furnished
money , and have expended the same in erecting and furnishing
schoolhouses in their respectivedistricts, and where the indebted-
ness so incurred remains unpaid, and the present rate of taxation

every year hereafter, untila sum sufficienthas been raised to pay

§ 2. Said tax should be levied and collectedas provided by law

That no tax shallbe leviedor collectedfrom the property of colored
persons to pay any indebtedness incurred for the benefitof schools
for white children .

classification.title: Agricultural and Mechanical College Provisions
classification.summary: This statute outlines provisions for the Agricultural and Mechanical College, detailing how county superintendents should inform common school districts about competitive examinations and the benefits of the college. It specifies that teachers or prospective teachers, up to four from each county, can be admitted under the same conditions as other beneficiaries, with appointments vested in county superintendents. The president of the college is required to mail informational circulars to county superintendents for posting in schoolhouses. The act also declares an emergency to allow for immediate implementation due to travel cost differences.

The focus is on educational opportunities and administrative procedures for common schools and prospective college students. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: INSTITUTION FOR THE EDUCATION OF THE BLIND
classification.summary: This statute outlines the appointment and duties of a board of visitors for the Institution for the Education of the Blind in Louisville, Kentucky. It details how vacancies are filled, the oath of office, the election of a president and secretary, the board's power to receive property, and its control over the institution's building, grounds, and management. The board can employ staff, fix their pay, prescribe duties, and remove them at pleasure. It also has the authority to set the course of education, rules for student government and discipline, and tuition fees for out-of-state pupils, while admitting in-state pupils without charge.

classification.title: An act of March 27, 1884, appropriating $20,000 for the erection of a separate building or buildings to be used for, and devoted to, the sole and exclusive occupation of the colored blind children of this Commonwealth.
classification.summary: This statute appropriates funds for the construction of separate buildings for "colored blind children" and mandates that they receive equal benefits and privileges as white blind children, but under the same general management and superintendent. It also mentions that the blind children of both races shall be under the same general management and superintendent.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored blind children' and mandates separate buildings for them, which is a clear indicator of segregation based on race. While it states they should receive equal benefits, the establishment of separate facilities is a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The mention of 'blind children of both races' under the same management further reinforces the context of racial segregation within an educational institution. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law in the education category.

classification.title: An Act to Establish an Institution for the Education of Colored Deaf Mutes
classification.summary: This act establishes a separate institution for the education of colored deaf mutes at Danville, Kentucky. It mandates that this institution be under the same board of commissioners as the institution for white deaf mutes, but explicitly states that the two races shall be kept entirely separate and distinct. An appropriation of $5,000 is made to prepare separate buildings for the colored deaf mutes.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states the need to provide for 'colored deaf and dumb children' and that 'the co-education of the two races is both inexpedient and unadvisable.' It then enacts legislation to establish a separate institution for colored deaf mutes, explicitly stating that 'the two races shall be forever kept entirely separate and distinct from each other.' This clearly indicates a Jim Crow law due to its mandated racial segregation in education.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the Kentucky Institute for Deaf Mutes.
classification.summary: This act appropriates funds for the Kentucky Institute for Deaf Mutes and mandates the admission of colored deaf and dumb pupils on the same terms as white pupils. It specifies that the colored department will receive the same per capita support and an additional annual appropriation for officers and teachers. Crucially, it states that both races will be under the same management and superintendent, with the board of commissioners for the white institution overseeing the colored department. It also notes that existing officers and employees will not receive additional pay for services to colored mutes.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored deaf and dumb' and 'white deaf and dumb,' indicating a racial distinction in the provision of education. While it states they are to be admitted 'on the same terms' and receive the 'same per capita for support,' the very act of creating a separate department for 'colored deaf and dumb' and placing it under the control of the white institution's board of commissioners reflects the segregationist policies characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The phrase 'separate but equal' is implied by the structure of the law, even if the intent was not truly equal. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because of the explicit racial language and the establishment of separate, though managed together, educational provisions.

Frankfort,Ky .,April 15, 1896.
To County Superintendents, Trustees, Teachers and Others, Interested
in the Betterment of Our Schools :
I here publish a listof the officialblanks most used to conduct
the business of our common schools;and following,the forms also.
Some of those named are not given herein but they are printed and
sent out by the State office. Some new forms have been added and
allhave been renumbered to enable us to group them under conveni-
ent general heads .
These forms have been prepared with great care as to theirlegal
bearings, and are thought to be up to date, so far as the law has
been construed by the Appellate Court . They will be a reliable
guide to the lessexperienced school officials, and will, we hope , con-
tribute materially to the efficientadministration of public school
business,and assistin making the system as nearly uniform as pos-
siblehroughout the State, preventing confusion and avoiding liti-
gation .
In most cases reference is made to the law giving riseto the
forms presented . Respectfully ,
W. J. DAVIDSON ,
Chairman State Board of Education .
